items.subject: architecture
items.topic: how the skyscraper became possible with steel-frame construction
items.transcript: Think about how tall a building can be if you stack heavy stone walls. Not very tall at all. In a traditional masonry building, the walls themselves carry the entire weight of the structure. As you add floors, the lower walls have to bear more and more load, so they must get thicker and thicker. By the time you reach ten or twelve stories, the ground-floor walls would be so massive they'd swallow most of the usable space. That's the wall they had to break through, and the answer came from steel. In the eighteen-eighties, engineers in Chicago began constructing buildings around an internal skeleton of steel beams and columns. This is called the steel frame. The crucial idea is that the frame, not the walls, carries the load. The exterior walls become a thin curtain, hung onto the frame like a coat on a rack. They keep out the weather, but they hold up nothing. The Home Insurance Building, completed in Chicago in eighteen eighty-five, is usually credited as the first true skyscraper using this method. Once the frame carried the weight, the limiting factor was no longer the strength of the walls but practical concerns like wind and elevators. So height became a question of engineering, not of masonry. This shift didn't just make taller buildings possible. It transformed the look of the modern city, allowing the glass-and-steel towers we now take for granted. The wall stopped being a support and became a skin.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How steel-frame construction made the skyscraper possible
options.B: The history of the city of Chicago in the eighteen-eighties
options.C: Why stone walls must get thicker on lower floors
options.D: How elevators allowed buildings to become much taller

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整段讲座的核心是钢框架结构如何让承重从墙体转移到内部钢骨架，从而使摩天大楼成为可能。B选项过于宽泛，芝加哥只是事件发生地，并非主题。C选项是讲座前半段用来说明传统砌体局限的一个细节，过于狭窄。D选项利用了讲座中提到的电梯一词，但电梯只是被顺带提及，不是主旨。干扰项类型：B是wrong_topic，C是too_narrow，D是surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what role do the exterior walls play in a steel-frame building?

options.A: They carry most of the weight of the upper floors
options.B: They form a thin curtain and hold up nothing
options.C: They must grow thicker toward the bottom of the building
options.D: They replace the steel columns on the ground floor

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说在钢框架建筑中，外墙变成一层薄薄的幕墙，像挂在架子上的外套，挡风雨但不承重。A选项与讲座相反，承重的是框架而非墙。C选项描述的是传统砌体建筑墙体的特点，被讲座用作对比，并非钢框架外墙。D选项是讲座未提及且与框架承重逻辑相悖的说法。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，C是wrong_detail，D是topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe stacking heavy stone walls at the start of the lecture?

options.A: To praise the durability of traditional masonry buildings
options.B: To claim that stone is stronger than steel for tall towers
options.C: To set up the problem that steel-frame construction solved
options.D: To describe the appearance of the modern glass-and-steel city

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授开头描述堆叠石墙是为了引出传统建筑高度受限这一难题，进而说明钢框架如何解决它。A选项错误，教授并非赞美砌体的耐久性，而是指出其局限。B选项与讲座相反，讲座说明钢框架优于厚墙。D选项是讲座结尾才出现的内容，与开头石墙描述的目的无关。干扰项类型：A是wrong_purpose，B是over_inference，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the height of skyscrapers after the steel frame was adopted?

options.A: It was still strictly limited by the thickness of the walls
options.B: It could no longer be increased beyond about twelve stories
options.C: It depended mainly on the quality of the stone available
options.D: It became chiefly a matter of engineering rather than masonry

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说一旦框架承重，限制因素就不再是墙的强度，而是风和电梯等工程问题，因此高度成为工程问题而非砌体问题。A选项与讲座相反，墙的厚度不再是限制。B选项把传统砌体的十到十二层上限错误地套到了钢框架时代。C选项重提石材质量，但钢框架时代石材已不再是关键。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，C是over_generalization。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: For most of history, a piece of music existed only as a performance. It happened once, in a room, and then it was gone. Composers wrote scores so that performers could recreate the work, but the score was just a set of instructions. The actual sound vanished the moment the players stopped. Recording technology changed that completely. Once sound could be captured and replayed, music became a fixed object that could be studied, edited, and perfected. And this didn't just preserve performances. It began to change how composers and artists thought about the work itself. Consider the recording studio in the nineteen-sixties. Artists started using the studio not as a place to document a live performance, but as an instrument in its own right. They layered tracks recorded at different times, spliced together the best moments from many takes, and added effects that no live ensemble could produce. A finished recording might be a performance that never actually happened in one continuous moment. The Beatles, for instance, created songs in the studio that they could not reproduce on stage at all. This marks a real shift. Composition was no longer only about writing notes for live players. It became about shaping recorded sound directly. The recording stopped being a copy of the music and became the music itself. And that idea still shapes how popular music is made today, where the recording is usually the original work, and any live version is the imitation.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why live performances sound better than studio recordings
options.B: How recording technology changed the way music was created
options.C: The career of the Beatles during the nineteen-sixties
options.D: How musical scores are written down for performers

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。讲座主旨是录音技术如何改变了音乐创作本身——从为现场演奏写谱转向直接塑造录制的声音。A选项与讲座相反，讲座并未说现场演奏更好。C选项中Beatles只是一个例证，过于狭窄。D选项利用了讲座中提到的乐谱一词，但乐谱只是背景对比，不是主题。干扰项类型：A是wrong_topic，C是too_narrow，D是surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did artists in the nineteen-sixties begin to use the recording studio?

options.A: As a way to write down scores for live performers
options.B: As a place to document a single live performance faithfully
options.C: As an instrument in its own right for shaping sound
options.D: As a method to make recordings sound exactly like concerts

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授明确说六十年代的艺术家开始把录音棚当作一种独立的乐器来使用，分层录音、拼接片段、加入现场无法实现的效果。A选项与录音棚的用途无关，把录谱混入。B选项恰恰是讲座说他们不再这样做的旧方式。D选项与讲座相反，他们制作的是现场无法重现的声音。干扰项类型：A是topic_adjacent，B是reversed_logic，D是wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the Beatles?

options.A: To argue that the Beatles invented recording technology
options.B: To compare the Beatles with classical composers of the past
options.C: To describe the layering of tracks done in the studio
options.D: To give an example of music that could not be performed live

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授举Beatles为例，是要说明他们在录音棚里创作出根本无法在舞台上重现的歌曲，从而印证创作重心转向录制声音。A选项过度推断，讲座并未说他们发明了录音技术。B选项错误，此处并非与古典作曲家作比较。C选项只是复述了studio中做了什么，而非解释提到Beatles的目的。干扰项类型：A是over_inference，B是wrong_purpose，C是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a live version of a modern pop song?

options.A: It is usually treated as an imitation of the recording
options.B: It is always superior to the studio recording
options.C: It is the original work that the recording copies
options.D: It can never be performed by the original artists

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授在结尾说如今录音通常是原作，而现场版本是模仿，由此可推断现代流行歌曲的现场版被视为录音的模仿。B选项过度夸大，讲座并未说现场总是更好。C选项与讲座相反，原作是录音而非现场。D选项太绝对，讲座只说某些作品现场难以重现，并非永远无法演出。干扰项类型：B是over_generalization，C是reversed_logic，D是topic_adjacent。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: Impressionism emerged in France in the eighteen-seventies, and one of its boldest moves was simply leaving the studio. Earlier painters worked indoors, building up carefully finished pictures under steady, controlled light. The Impressionists wanted something else. They wanted to capture a fleeting moment, the way light actually looks on a particular afternoon. So they carried their easels outside and painted directly from nature, a practice known by the French phrase 'en plein air.' This wasn't just a change of location. It forced a change in how they understood color. Outdoors, light shifts constantly, and colors are never pure or isolated. A white wall in shadow isn't simply gray. It picks up blue from the sky and warm tones from nearby surfaces. Painters working in a studio, mixing colors slowly on a palette, would smooth all of that away. But out in the open, the Impressionists noticed that shadows are full of color. To capture this, they applied small strokes of bright, often unmixed paint side by side. Stand close, and the strokes look like a confusing patchwork. Step back, and your eye blends them into a shimmering whole. Two new paint tubes and portable easels, recently manufactured, made this outdoor work practical for the first time. The result was a fresh, luminous way of seeing. By painting outdoors, the Impressionists weren't just choosing new subjects. They were rethinking what color is and how the eye perceives it.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The invention of portable easels and ready-made paint tubes
options.B: Why earlier painters preferred to work inside a studio
options.C: How painting outdoors changed the Impressionists' view of color
options.D: The history of art movements in France in the eighteen-seventies

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座主旨是印象派走出画室到户外作画，以及这一做法如何改变了他们对色彩的理解。A选项是讲座中支持户外作画的一个技术细节，过于狭窄。B选项是用来对比的背景，并非主题。D选项过于宽泛，讲座只聚焦印象派而非整个法国艺术史。干扰项类型：A是too_narrow，B是too_narrow，D是wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Impressionists notice about shadows when painting outdoors?

options.A: They were always a pure, neutral gray
options.B: They disappeared entirely in bright afternoon light
options.C: They were easiest to paint slowly on a palette indoors
options.D: They were full of color reflected from the surroundings

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授明确说在户外，印象派发现阴影里充满色彩，比如阴影中的白墙会反射天空的蓝色和附近物体的暖色。A选项与讲座相反，讲座特意指出阴影不是单纯的灰色。B选项是讲座未提及的说法。C选项把室内调色板的做法错误地安到了户外观察上。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，C是wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe stepping close to and then back from the painting?

options.A: To explain how separate strokes of color blend in the viewer's eye
options.B: To argue that Impressionist paintings should only be viewed up close
options.C: To prove that the Impressionists could not mix colors well
options.D: To describe how studio painters finished their pictures

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授描述凑近看是杂乱笔触、退后看则融为一体，是为了说明观众的眼睛会把并置的纯色笔触在视觉上混合起来。B选项过度推断，讲座并未说只能近看。C选项错误，分开上色是有意的技法，而非能力不足。D选项与此处描述的目的无关，画室画家是对比对象。干扰项类型：B是over_inference，C是wrong_purpose，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studio painting compared with painting outdoors?

options.A: It captured fleeting changes in light more accurately
options.B: It tended to smooth away the subtle colors found in shadows
options.C: It relied on small strokes of bright, unmixed paint
options.D: It was the main subject the Impressionists wanted to depict

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说在画室里慢慢调色会把阴影中那些微妙的色彩抹平，由此可推断画室作画倾向于消除这些色彩。A选项与讲座相反，户外作画才更能捕捉瞬息的光线变化。C选项把印象派户外技法错误地归给了画室。D选项错误，画室作画是被对比的对象，而非印象派想要描绘的题材。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，C是topic_adjacent，D是over_generalization。

items.subject: biology
items.transcript: Tardigrades are tiny animals, barely half a millimeter long, often called water bears because of the way they lumber along under a microscope. They live almost everywhere, in moss, in soil, in the deep sea. But what makes them famous is their astonishing ability to survive conditions that would kill almost anything else. Tardigrades have been frozen near absolute zero, heated past the boiling point of water, exposed to enormous doses of radiation, and even taken into the vacuum of outer space, and they have come back to life. So how do they do it? The secret is a process called cryptobiosis. When their environment dries out, a tardigrade doesn't just slow down. It essentially shuts off. It pulls in its legs, curls into a tiny ball called a tun, and expels almost all the water from its body. Its metabolism drops to a fraction of one percent of normal. In this state, it isn't really alive in the usual sense, but it isn't dead either. It can stay this way for years, even decades. Then, when water returns, the tardigrade rehydrates and walks away as if nothing happened. Researchers think a special sugar replaces the lost water and protects the animal's cells from damage. Studying how tardigrades pull this off could teach us how to preserve cells, tissues, and maybe one day even organs for medicine. These tiny survivors have a lot to teach us.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why tardigrades are commonly known as water bears
options.B: How researchers send tiny animals into outer space
options.C: The many different habitats where tardigrades can be found
options.D: How tardigrades survive extreme environments through cryptobiosis

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座主旨是缓步动物如何通过隐生这一过程在极端环境中存活。A选项只是开头解释俗名的一个细节，过于狭窄。B选项利用了讲座中提到的外太空，但研究方法不是主题。C选项是讲座顺带提到的栖息地，并非核心。干扰项类型：A是too_narrow，B是surface_word_trap，C是too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a tardigrade's body during cryptobiosis?

options.A: It curls into a ball and expels almost all of its water
options.B: It grows larger to store extra water for later
options.C: It speeds up its metabolism to fight the dry conditions
options.D: It splits into several smaller animals to survive

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授明确说在隐生状态下，缓步动物收起腿、蜷成一个叫tun的小球，并排出体内几乎所有的水分。B选项与讲座相反，它是排水而非储水变大。C选项与讲座相反，它的代谢降到正常的极小一部分，而非加快。D选项是讲座未提及的说法。干扰项类型：B是reversed_logic，C是reversed_logic，D是topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that tardigrades have been taken into the vacuum of outer space?

options.A: To suggest that tardigrades originally came from space
options.B: To give a striking example of the extreme conditions they survive
options.C: To explain how researchers first discovered cryptobiosis
options.D: To describe the typical habitats where tardigrades live

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授列举外太空真空，是为了用一个极端的例子来突出缓步动物惊人的生存能力。A选项过度推断，讲座从未暗示它们来自太空。C选项错误，外太空的例子与隐生如何被发现无关。D选项错误，太空不是它们的栖息地，讲座说的栖息地是苔藓、土壤、深海。干扰项类型：A是over_inference，C是wrong_purpose，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying tardigrades?

options.A: It has already failed to produce any useful results
options.B: It is mainly useful for exploring distant planets
options.C: It could help develop ways to preserve cells and tissues
options.D: It shows that tardigrades cannot survive on Earth

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说研究缓步动物如何做到这一点，可能教会我们如何保存细胞、组织乃至器官，由此可推断这类研究有助于发展细胞和组织的保存方法。A选项与讲座相反，讲座对前景持乐观态度。B选项把外太空的细节夸大成研究的主要用途。D选项与讲座相反，缓步动物本就生活在地球各处。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，D是over_generalization。
